content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 428 J STREET, STE 800, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662         322-5660          322-5901          322-6441


                                                    September 13, 1985


Daniel F. Bamberg, President
Mission Hills Democratic Club
405 West Washington St., No. 177
San Diego, CA 92103

                                                    Re: Our File No. A-85-174

Dear Mr. Bamberg:

     I apologize for the delay in responding to your letter of
August 6, 1985, which requested advice with respect to the cam-
paign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (Gov.
Code Sections 81000-91015). Specifically, you asked whether the
Mission Hills Democratic Club is receiving "contributions" as
defined by the Act.

     FPPC regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215 (copy enclosed)
defines the term "contribution" as "any monetary or nonmonetary
payment made for political purposes for which full and adequate
consideration is not made to the donor." The regulation also
states that a payment is made for "political purposes" if it is:

          For the purpose of influencing or attempting to
          influence the action of the voters for or against
          the nomination or election of a candidate or
          candidates, or the qualification or passage of
          any measure.

     As I told you during our telephone conversation today, all of the
activities described in your letter fall within this definition
and, therefore, the Mission Hills Democratic Club is a "recipient
committee" pursuant to Section 82013(a) of the Political Reform
Act. In addition, because the Club has received a charter from
the San Diego County Central Committee, it qualifies as "an
official committee of a political party" pursuant to subsection
(a)(2)(C) of Regulation 18215.

     Enclosed please find the FPPC Information Manual on Campaign
Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act along with the
disclosure forms the Club will need to file.

Daniel F. Bamberg
September 13, 1985
Page 2


     If you need additional information or assistance, please
do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 or the Commission's
Los Angeles office at (213) 620-5196.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible signature]
                                        Carla Wardlow
                                        Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

F P P MISSION HILLS DEMOCRATIC CLUB

AUG 8 12 sa PM-05 4255 West Washington Street, No. 177
San Diego, California 92103


August 6, 1985


Executive Director
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 "K" Street, Second Floor
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Advice Pursuant to F.P.P.C.
Regulation §18329(d)

Dear Executive Director:

I am the president of the recently formed Mission Hills
Democratic Club. We have 87 paid members and hope to
substantially increase our membership in the near future.

Our annual dues range from $25.00 to $100.00. However,
members are encouraged to donate at least $100.00 and some
members have paid as much as $500.00 to join.

It is our intention to publish a "monthly newsletter". This
newsletter will be sent to all registered Democrats within a
certain geographical area...an area that includes over 5,000
registered Democrats. Note the newsletter will be sent to all
registered Democrats, not just to members of the Club. It is
also our intention to hold "nominating conventions" and to
endorse candidates that will appear on the ballot of voters
within our boundaries.

Once a candidate receives the endorsement of the Mission
Hills Democratic Club, the Club intends to support that candidate
in our newsletter. Also, to the extent possible, our Club
members will walk precincts on behalf of candidates, endorsed by
the Club. Finally, should the Club have funds available, we
intend to contribute to the campaigns of Club-endorsed
candidates.

Are we a committee as described in Government Code §82013?
If we are a committee, what are our reporting obligations?

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 6, 1985
Page Two


Originally I did not think the Mission Hills Democratic Club
would be covered by the Political Reform Act of 1974 because we
are not associated with any particular campaign. However, I find
the language of Government Code §82015, defining a
"contribution", so broad as to possibly include the activities of
the Mission Hills Democratic Club.

I am enclosing a copy of our Bylaws. Note that we have
received a "Charter" from the San Diego County Central Committee.

To date we have made no political type expenditures, having
only just become organized. We intend to send out our first
Mission Hills Democratic Club newsletter in mid-September, 1985.

Please contact me at my office, [(619) 296-5077], if there
are any questions I can answer to assist you in this matter.

I appreciate your attention to this matter and look forward
to your response.

Respectfully,

MISSION HILLS DEMOCRATIC CLUB

Daniel F. Bamberg
President

DFB:yds
Enclosure

BYLAWS

OF

MISSION HILLS
DEMOCRATIC CLUB OF SAN DIEGO COUNTY

ARTICLE I

NAME

The name of the organization shall be the"Mission Hills
Democratic Club of San Diego County".

ARTICLE II

GEOGRAPHICAL DESIGNATION FOR RESIDENT MEMBERSHIP

Mission Hills, as defined for purposes of the Mission Hills
Democratic Club, shall be bounded on the North by Interstate 8,
on the East by Interstate 163, on the South by Laurel Street and
on the West by Interstate 5.

ARTICLE III

PURPOSE

The purpose of the Mission Hills Democratic Club is to
foster the ideals of the Democratic Party by stimulating an
active interest in the Democratic Party; providing a constructive
role for the volunteer in Democratic Politics; and contributing
to party leadership and responsibility, promoting harmony within
the party, supporting the party platform and electing democratic
candidates in all elections, partisan and non-partisan, local,
State-wide and national.

ARTICLE IV

MEMBERSHIP

Section 1.     Types of Membership.
There shall be three categories of membership
in the Mission Hills Democratic Club: resident, associate
and sustaining.

Section 2.     Resident Membership.
A person residing in Mission Hills who is a
voter, registered as a Democrat or, if unable to register to
vote, has expressed the intent to register to vote as a
Democrat, shall be eligible for resident membership status
in the Mission Hills Democratic Club.

1

Section 3.  Non-Resident Membership.
     A person residing in San Diego County, but
not residing in Mission Hills who is a voter, registered as
a Democrat or, if unable to register to vote, has expressed
the intent to register to vote as a Democrat, shall be
eligible for non-resident membership status in the Mission
Hills Democratic Club.

Section 4.  Associate Membership.
     A person not registered as a Democrat shall
be eligible for associate membership status in the Mission
Hills Democratic Club, but shall be ineligible to vote.

ARTICLE V

DUES

Section 1.  Amount.
     The annual dues for each category of
membership may be established from time to time by a
majority vote of the general membership. As of January 1,
1985, and until such time as the general membership shall
establish a different amount, the annual dues of membership
in the Mission Hills Democratic Club shall be as follows:

     a.  Resident Membership ........ $25.00
     b.  Non-Resident Membership .... $50.00
     c.  Associate Membership ....... $100.00

Section 2.  When Due.
     Annual dues for membership in the Mission
Hills Democratic Club shall be paid upon becoming a member
and on or before February 1, of each year thereafter.

Section 3.  Hardship.
     Upon application to the Membership Committee
and upon a majority vote of the membership, an applicant for
membership may initially be admitted to the Mission Hills
Democratic Club for an amount less than that established by
these Bylaws or that set by the general membership pursuant
to Article V, Section 1. A member, admitted to membership
pursuant to this Section, shall pay annual dues as specified
in Article V, Section 1, unless application again be made to
the Membership Committee and the general membership approves
of some different amount.


2

ARTICLE VI

VOTING

Section 1.     Eligibility To Vote.
Only those resident and non-resident
memberships, current in their payment of dues may vote.

Section 2.     One Vote Per Membership.
A membership held in the name of one or more
individuals shall be entitled to one vote.

Section 3.     Proxy And Absentee Voting.
No proxy voting or absentee ballots shall be
permitted for endorsement of candidates for political
office. Proxy voting and absentee ballots shall be accepted
for proposed changes in the club's Bylaws, Resolutions and
elections.

Section 4.     Majority Vote.
Except for amendments to these Bylaws, which
shall require a two-thirds vote, all other votes taken of
the general membership shall a require a majority vote of
the members then present and voting. For purposes of this
Section, a member casting an absentee ballot or a proxy
shall be considered present and voting.

ARTICLE VII

ELECTED OFFICERS

Section 1.     Elected Officers.
The elected officers of the Mission Hills
Democratic Club shall be the President, Executive Vice-
President, Administrative Vice-President, Secretary and
Treasurer.

Elected officers shall hold office for two
years from the close of the June general meeting and serve
until a new slate of officers take over after the June
general meeting two years following their election.

Section 2.     Duties Of Elected Officers.

(a) President. The President shall be
responsible for the club's public relations, shall
preside over all meetings of the club and of the
Executive Committee and shall perform all other duties
which are incidental to the office of President.

(b) Executive Vice-President. The Executive
Vice-President shall take over the duties of the club
in the absence of the President and shall be

3

responsible for the club's political action program and
shall serve as chair of the nominations committee.

(c) Administrative Vice-President. The
Administrative Vice-President shall be responsible to
plan programs for the general meetings of the club and
shall serve as chair of the Events Committee.

(d) Secretary. The Secretary shall keep the
minutes of the general meetings and Executive
Committee meetings, conduct correspondence, maintain
the club's records and be responsible for internal
communications of the club including the club's
newsletter and telephone communications program, and
shall chair the Membership Committee.

(e) Treasurer. The Treasurer shall receive
and hold all club funds. The Treasurer shall prepare a
quarterly report of the club's financial situation for
presentation at the general meeting, shall make the
books and all other financial records of the club
available for audit in the month of May of each year,
shall be responsible for the collection of dues and the
fundraising activities of the club, and shall chair the
Finance Committee.

Section 3. Elections.
At a March general meeting the President
shall name four members to serve on the Nominating Committee
chaired by the Administrative Vice-President. The
Nominating Committee shall select at least one candidate for
each elective office and report their selections at the
general meeting in June.

This report shall constitute nominations, and
the floor shall be open for further nominations.

Those members nominated at the March general
meeting shall be voted upon at the June general meeting by
secret ballot. Those nominees who receive a majority vote
shall be declared elected. If no person receives a majority
of votes cast, a run-off shall be held between those two
persons receiving the greatest number of votes and the
person who thereupon receives a majority of votes cast shall
be declared elected.

Section 4. Replacement.
Should any officer fail to attend three
regularly scheduled general meetings in succession without
reasonable cause, the office shall be declared vacant by the
Executive Committee.

4

Any vacant elective office shall be filled by
appointment by the Executive Committee subject to
ratification by the membership at the next general meeting,
provided that the membership is notified in the manner
specified in Article IX, of the Bylaws. If the club does
not ratify the choice of the Executive Committee, then
nomination shall be accepted from the floor at the meeting.
If more than one person is nominated, then election will be
by secret ballot and the person receiving majority of votes
cast shall be declared elected.

Section 5.      Appointed Officers.
The President shall appoint a parliamentarian
and any other officers not otherwise specified by these
Bylaws as required for the general well being of the club.

ARTICLE VIII

STANDING COMMITTEES

The Mission Hills Democratic Club shall have the
following standing committees: Finance, (and) Membership;

Section 1.      Finance.
The Finance Committee shall be chaired by the
Treasurer and comprised of at least five other members
appointed by the Treasurer and ratified by the Executive
Committee. The Finance Committee shall be responsible for
all fundraising activities and for insuring that the Mission
Hills Democratic Club adheres to sound financial practices.

Section 2.      Membership.
The Membership Committee shall be chaired by
the Secretary and comprised of at least five other members
appointed by the Secretary and ratified by the Executive
Committee. The Membership Committee shall be responsible
for maintaining a current list of the membership, obtaining
new members, and publishing and distributing a
Mission Hills Democratic Club Newsletter to the membership
at least four times per year.

Section 3.      Events.
The Events Committee shall be chaired by the
Administrative Vice-President and comprised of at least five
other members appointed by the Administrative Vice-President
and ratified by the Executive Committee. The Events
Committee shall be responsible for scheduling at least four
meetings per year for the general membership and such other
meetings or events as the Events Committee shall from time
to time schedule.

5

Section 4.     Executive.
The Executive Committee shall be chaired by
the President and comprised of all elected officers. The
Executive Committee shall be responsible for carrying out
the club's business between general meetings in a manner not
inconsistent with actions taken at general meetings and any
other duties specified in these Bylaws or in Roberts' Rules
of Order, Newly Revised.

The President shall call a meeting of the
Executive Committee at least once a month, however a meeting
may be called by a majority of elected officers. The quorum
at an Executive Committee meeting is a majority of elected
officers.

If immediate action is needed, the Executive
Committee may act by telephonic vote, but such actions are
subject to ratification at an Executive committee meeting to
be called as soon thereafter as feasible.

ARTICLE VIX

MEETINGS

Section 1.     General Meetings.
There shall be at least four general meetings
of the membership per year. General meetings shall be held
at a time and place specified by the Executive Committee.

Section 2.     Quorum.
A quorum at a general meeting shall be ten
percent (10%) of the club's resident and associate members
in good standing.

Section 3.     Parliamentary Authority.
Roberts Rules of Order, Newly Revised, shall
be the parliamentary authority for the club on all questions
not covered by the Bylaws.

ARTICLE X

NOTICE

At least seven days prior to the general meeting, the
Secretary shall cause to be deposited into the regular United
States mail a newsletter or notice addressed to each member in
good standing with the club. The newsletter or notice shall
specify the time, place and date of the general meeting, proposed
changes in the Bylaws, resolutions, endorsements and elections
which are to be voted upon at the upcoming general meeting.


6

ARTICLE XI

CANDIDATE ENDORSEMENT

The club may endorse and/or contribute financially to
Democratic candidates in partisan elections or any candidate in a
non-partisan election, as directed by a majority vote at a
general meeting provided that notice is given as specified in
Article IX, of these Bylaws.

ARTICLE XII

AFFILIATION

The club shall affiliate as an associate member of the
San Diego County Democratic Central Committee and pay the current
annual fee.

ARTICLE XIII

AMENDMENT

These Bylaws may be amended by a two-thirds vote at a
general meeting after such amendment has been presented at the
general meeting the preceding vote and notice is provided as
specified in Article IX of these Bylaws.

ARTICLE XIV

ADOPTION

These Bylaws shall become effective immediately upon their
adoption and shall supercede any other Constitution, Bylaws, and
any other document of this club with which it is inconsistent.

Adopted on March 14th, 1985 at the
first regular meeting of the
Mission Hills Democratic Club.

7

sections.question: Specifically, you asked whether the Mission Hills Democratic Club is receiving "contributions" as defined by the Act.
sections.conclusion: As I told you during our telephone conversation today, all of the activities described in your letter fall within this definition and, therefore, the Mission Hills Democratic Club is a "recipient committee" pursuant to Section 82013(a) of the Political Reform Act. In addition, because the Club has received a charter from the San Diego County Central Committee, it qualifies as "an official committee of a political party" pursuant to subsection (a)(2)(C) of Regulation 18215.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Mission Hills Democratic Club, a recently formed Democratic Party organization that collects membership dues, publishes a newsletter to registered Democrats, holds nomination conventions, endorses candidates, and intends to contribute to endorsed candidates' campaigns, is receiving "contributions" as defined by the Political Reform Act and what are its reporting obligations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Mission Hills Democratic Club is a recipient committee under the Political Reform Act because its described activities fall within the definition of making payments for political purposes. Additionally, as a chartered official committee of a political party, it must comply with applicable disclosure and filing requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated August 6, 1985) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 13, 1985). The incoming request includes the club's bylaws as enclosure. FPPC response clearly identifies the question asked and provides definitive conclusion. Minor OCR errors present in original document (e.g., 'connnittee' with triple-n) but content is clear.

QUESTION: Specifically, you asked whether the Mission Hills Democratic Club is receiving "contributions" as defined by the Act.

CONCLUSION: As I told you during our telephone conversation today, all of the activities described in your letter fall within this definition and, therefore, the Mission Hills Democratic Club is a "recipient committee" pursuant to Section 82013(a) of the Political Reform Act. In addition, because the Club has received a charter from the San Diego County Central Committee, it qualifies as "an official committee of a political party" pursuant to subsection (a)(2)(C) of Regulation 18215.

Code Section 18215 (copy enclosed) defines the term "contribution" as "any monetary or nonmonetary payment made for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor." The regulation also states that a payment is made for "political purposes" if it is: For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure. As I told you during our telephone conversation today, all of the activities described in your letter fall within this definition and, therefore, the Mission Hills Democratic Club is a "recipient committee" pursuant to Section 82013(a) of the Political Reform Act. embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the Mission Hills Democratic Club's inquiry confirming that the club's membership dues, newsletter distribution to registered Democrats, candidate endorsements, and intended contributions to endorsed candidates constitute activities receiving "contributions" under the Political Reform Act. The club is classified as a recipient committee and official party committee subject to campaign disclosure requirements and must file appropriate forms.
